run.output:
Query returned 59142609 rows
Execution time: 49 ms

validation.comparison_result: 1 row differs: Row 2 (N,O group) has precision error in sum_disc_price: expected 1058580922144.9638, actual 1058580922144.9651 (delta=0.0013). Other 3 rows match. Rows returned: 4 (expected 4).

Q1.notes: Execution time is 49ms vs. GenDB baseline of 48ms (target: sub-50ms). Row count correct (4). One precision error in aggregation: sum_disc_price differs by 0.0013 in the N,O group due to floating point accumulation error.

Q1.bottleneck: Floating point precision in sum_disc_price computation. The accumulated result differs from ground truth in the last 4 decimal places (1058580922144.9638 vs 1058580922144.9651).
Q1.root_cause: Double-precision floating point arithmetic accumulates rounding errors when summing large numbers across 59M rows. The formula `price * (1.0 - disc)` is computed on per-row basis and accumulated; order of operations and intermediate rounding in double math (53-bit mantissa) causes divergence from expected value. The computation is mathematically correct but numerically unstable.

analysis.overall: Q1 compiles and executes at near-baseline speed (49ms vs 48ms baseline, 2% overhead). All structural requirements met: 64-thread parallel aggregation, compact hash table, mmap I/O with sequential hints, dictionary encoding for strings. However, query produces WRONG RESULTS due to floating point precision loss. The discrepancy is small (0.0013 ULP in a sum ~10^15), but violates correctness requirement. Root cause: double-precision accumulation cannot guarantee exact TPC-H result precision. Must use Kahan summation, extended precision, or decimal arithmetic.

critical_fixes.issue: Floating point precision error: sum_disc_price differs by 0.0013 (1058580922144.9638 expected vs 1058580922144.9651 actual). Query produces WRONG RESULTS.
critical_fixes.fix: Implement Kahan summation algorithm for all SUM operations. Kahan summation maintains a running compensation term to reduce rounding errors and achieves IEEE 754 level correctness for large accumulations.
critical_fixes.description: Replace direct += accumulation with Kahan summation: maintain (sum, compensation) pair. For each value, compute: y = value - compensation; t = sum + y; compensation = (t - sum) - y; sum = t. This reduces error to O(eps) instead of O(n*eps). Apply to sum_qty, sum_base_price, sum_disc_price, sum_charge, sum_discount. See q1.cpp lines 235-240 (direct accumulation) and 271-276 (merge).

performance_optimizations.description: Currently, date filtering (col_shipdate[j] > CUTOFF_DATE) is done per-row in a scalar loop (q1.cpp line 218). With 59M rows, this is 59M branch predictions. Use AVX2/AVX-512 to process 8 or 16 dates per iteration with vector comparison, reducing branch density by 8-16x. Load 8 int32_t dates, broadcast CUTOFF_DATE to all lanes, use pcmpd (compare packed dwords), extract mask. Apply mask to subsequent aggregate operations.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 15-25% reduction in filter loop latency. Filtering is O(n) but at 49ms total, filtering + aggregation dominate. SIMD filtering could save ~5-8ms.

performance_optimizations.description: Current mmap uses MADV_SEQUENTIAL but no explicit prefetch instructions. Loop processes col_shipdate, col_quantity, col_extendedprice, etc. sequentially. Intel prefetchers are good but explicit prefetching (e.g., _mm_prefetch) in the aggregate loop can hide memory latency. Prefetch 16-32 lines ahead in each column. Pattern: for j in 0..end: _mm_prefetch((char*)&col_shipdate[j+16], _MM_HINT_T1); for j in 0..end: process row j.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% speedup. With HDD storage and 7 mmapped columns, reducing memory stalls saves ~2-5ms.

performance_optimizations.technique: Zone map skipping for date predicate
performance_optimizations.description: The storage_design.json specifies a l_shipdate_sorted index on lineitem. The predicate l_shipdate <= CUTOFF_DATE (1998-09-02) filters 98.7% of rows. If zone maps (min/max per block) are available in metadata, entire blocks whose shipdate_max > CUTOFF_DATE can be skipped. This requires: (1) reading metadata.json to extract per-block zone maps for l_shipdate, (2) for each block, checking if block_max <= CUTOFF_DATE; if yes, accumulate; else skip. Current code loads ALL 60M rows; zone map skipping could reduce I/O by ~1-3% (since 98.7% rows are kept anyway).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 1-2% I/O reduction. Minimal impact on 49ms query (HDD already dominates network latency). More valuable for multiple runs.

performance_optimizations.technique: Loop unrolling and cache line prefetching
performance_optimizations.description: The thread-local aggregation loop (q1.cpp lines 216-247) processes one row per iteration. Hash table operations (operator[]) are inlined but still expensive due to Robin Hood probing. Unroll inner loop 2-4x: process 2-4 rows per iteration before hash operations. Combines loop overhead reduction with better prefetching of hash table entries and next row's column values.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 3-7% speedup from reduced loop overhead and better prefetch patterns. ~1-3ms savings.

performance_optimizations.technique: Sorted aggregation instead of hash aggregation
performance_optimizations.description: Q1's GROUP BY has only 6 groups (l_returnflag × l_linestatus: 3 flags × 2 statuses). The workload analysis identifies cardinality as 'very_low'. Current code uses hash aggregation (CompactHashTable with Robin Hood hashing). For 6 groups, sorted aggregation is superior: presort rows by (returnflag, linestatus), then stream aggregate. Eliminates hash table probing entirely. Implementation: (1) After filtering, extract (returnflag, linestatus) tuples, (2) radix-sort by these two bytes, (3) single-pass stream aggregate. Requires presort (O(n)) but eliminates hash table overhead (typically 10-20 cycles per insert).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-15% speedup. Sorted aggregation O(n) with cache-friendly sequential access. For 6 groups, avoids ~59M hash probes and associated cache misses.

summary: Q1 achieves baseline execution speed (49ms, comparable to 48ms GenDB target) with excellent parallelization on 64 cores, mmap-based columnar I/O, and compact hash table aggregation. However, correctness is BROKEN: floating point precision error in sum_disc_price column (delta=0.0013 in accumulated sum) fails TPC-H validation. CRITICAL FIX REQUIRED: Implement Kahan summation for all SUM operations to achieve IEEE 754 correctness. Once fixed, secondary optimizations include SIMD filtering (15-25% improvement), cache prefetching (5-10%), zone map skipping (1-2% I/O reduction), loop unrolling (3-7%), and sorted aggregation for low-cardinality GROUP BY (5-15%). Current speed is good; correctness must be addressed first.
